title: Chapter 7 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on Blocks, Procs, and Lambdas!

questions.question: What exactly is a Block in Ruby?
options: A chunk of code saved to a variable.
An anonymous (nameless) chunk of code passed directly into a method.
questions.explanation: A block is an anonymous chunk of code you attach straight to a method call.

questions.question: What happens to a Block after it finishes executing?
options: It is permanently saved as a Method.
It is saved to your computer's RAM.
It disappears completely because it has no name.
questions.explanation: Blocks are anonymous. Once they run, they vanish!

questions.question: Which Ruby keyword is used inside a method to pause the method and execute the block attached to it?
questions.explanation: The `yield` keyword yields control of the program over to the block.

questions.question: What happens if a method calls `yield`, but the user forgot to actually attach a block when calling the method?
options: It waits infinitely for a block to be attached.
It crashes the program with a LocalJumpError.
It automatically passes an empty block.
questions.explanation: If a method expects a block and none is provided, it critically fails.

questions.question: How can you safely write a method that uses `yield` only if a block is actually provided?
questions.explanation: `block_given?` is a magic Ruby method that returns true simply if the user attached a block.

questions.question: If your method says `yield("Alice")`, what must the attached block code look like to catch that word?
questions.explanation: Block parameters are always caught between vertical pipes `| |`.

questions.question: What is a 'Proc'?
options: A process running on the server.
A Block of code that has been saved into a variable name so it can be reused.
questions.explanation: Proc stands for Procedure. It lets you save a block of code so you don't violate the DRY rule.

questions.question: How do you actually execute the code inside a Proc after saving it?
questions.explanation: To trigger the block hidden inside the Proc or Lambda, you use `.call`.

questions.question: You can pass a Proc directly into an iterator like `.each` if you put an ampersand `&` in front of the variable name.
questions.explanation: Yes! `array.each(&my_proc)` translates the Proc back into an anonymous block for the method to use.

questions.question: What does a Lambda do?
options: It saves a chunk of code to a variable so it can be reused later.
questions.explanation: A Lambda is just the sibling of a Proc. It saves code to a variable and is executed via `.call`.

questions.question: In 90% of scenarios, what is the functional difference between a Proc and a Lambda?
options: There is absolutely no functional difference.
questions.explanation: Besides their strictness checks, they are identical twins.

questions.question: Identify the 'Stabby Lambda' syntax:
questions.explanation: The Stabby Lambda uses the dash-arrow `->` to mimic JavaScript arrow functions.

questions.question: What happens if you pass the WRONG amount of arguments into a Lambda?
options: It crashes instantly with an ArgumentError.
questions.explanation: Lambdas are STRICT. They demand the exact number of incoming arguments, perfectly mimicking actual methods.

questions.question: What happens if you pass the WRONG amount of arguments into a Proc?
options: It crashes instantly with an ArgumentError.
It replaces missing arguments with `nil` and silently continues.
questions.explanation: Procs are LAZY. If you don't give them exactly what they asked for, they just use `nil` and try to survive.

questions.question: If a Lambda hits the explicit `return` keyword, what does it do?
options: It permanently crashes the entire program.
It hands the data backward normally, allowing the parent method to continue executing its code.
It terminates the parent method completely.
questions.explanation: Lambdas correctly return flow to the method that called them.

questions.question: If a Proc hits the explicit `return` keyword, what does it do?
options: It permanently crashes the entire program.
It hands the data backward normally, allowing the parent method to continue executing its code.
It instantly terminates itself AND completely destroys the parent method it was called inside of.
questions.explanation: This is the most critical difference. Procs rip the parent method apart when they return, treating the code as if it was written directly on the method wall.

questions.question: Because of its safe parameter checking and standard return logic, professional developers prefer using slightly-strict _____ over Procs.
questions.explanation: Lambdas are nearly always the safer choice to prevent silent buggy failures.

questions.question: In the Stabby Lambda syntax `->(name) { puts title }`, where do the parameters go?
options: In parentheses `()` right next to the arrow.
questions.explanation: The arrow literally points at the parameters!

questions.question: The `.times` iterator creates an infinite loop in the background to work.
questions.explanation: It iterates exactly the number of times specified, using a `while` loop that yields counting up to that number.

questions.question: Which of these perfectly creates an anonymous block on a single line?
questions.explanation: Curly braces `{ }` replace the `do..end` keywords to create an inline block of code.
